subject: Physics
chapter: Thermal and Chemical Effects of Electric Current

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Thermal and Chemical Effects of Electric Current
questions.q: An iron strip $AC$ and a copper strip $AB$ are connected together at $A$. The junction $A$ is kept at $0^\circ C$, while the free ends $B$ and $C$ are held at $100^\circ C$. A potential difference exists between
options: two locations on the copper strip that are at the same temperature
the free ends $B$ and $C$
the two ends of the copper strip
the two ends of the iron strip
questions.solution:
Due to the Seebeck effect, a potential difference (thermo-emf) exists between the free ends $B$ and $C$ of the two dissimilar metals (iron and copper) because the junction $A$ is at $0^{\circ}C$ and the free ends are at $100^{\circ}C$.

Due to the Thomson effect, a potential difference exists across the ends of a single conductor if there is a temperature gradient along its length. Since end $A$ is at $0^{\circ}C$ and ends $B$ and $C$ are at $100^{\circ}C$, a potential difference exists between the two ends of the copper strip ($A$ and $B$) and between the two ends of the iron strip ($A$ and $C$).

No potential difference exists between two locations on the same metal if they are at the same temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Thermal and Chemical Effects of Electric Current
questions.q: For the silver–lead pair, the constants $a$ and $b$ are given as $2.50 \text{ }\mu\text{V }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $0.012 \text{ }\mu\text{V }^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$, respectively. If a silver–lead thermocouple has its colder junction kept at $0^\circ\text{C}$,
options: a neutral temperature will not exist
no current will be present in the thermocouple even if the junctions are held at different temperatures
a thermo-emf will not be generated even if the junctions are held at different temperatures
an inversion temperature will not exist
questions.solution:
The thermo-emf $E$ of a thermocouple with the cold junction at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and the hot junction at $t^\circ\text{C}$ is given by the relation:

$E = at + \dfrac{1}{2}bt^2$

The neutral temperature $t_n$ is the temperature of the hot junction at which the thermo-emf is maximum, which means $\dfrac{dE}{dt} = 0$.

$\dfrac{dE}{dt} = a + bt_n = 0 \implies t_n = -\dfrac{a}{b}$

Given $a = 2.50 \text{ } \mu\text{V }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $b = 0.012 \text{ } \mu\text{V }^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$:

$t_n = -\dfrac{2.50}{0.012} = -208.3^\circ\text{C}$

Since the colder junction is kept at $0^\circ\text{C}$, the hot junction must be at a temperature $t > 0^\circ\text{C}$. Because $t_n < 0^\circ\text{C}$, the hot junction can never reach the neutral temperature. Thus, a neutral temperature will not exist for this setup.

The inversion temperature $t_i$ is the temperature of the hot junction at which the thermo-emf becomes zero (other than $0^\circ\text{C}$).

$E = 0 \implies at_i + \dfrac{1}{2}bt_i^2 = 0 \implies t_i = -\dfrac{2a}{b} = 2t_n$

$t_i = 2 \times (-208.3^\circ\text{C}) = -416.6^\circ\text{C}$

Since this temperature is below absolute zero ($-273.15^\circ\text{C}$), it is physically impossible to achieve. Thus, an inversion temperature will not exist.

Therefore, both the neutral temperature and the inversion temperature will not exist.

Answer: a neutral temperature will not exist; an inversion temperature will not exist

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Thermal and Chemical Effects of Electric Current
questions.q: A current is passed through a combination of two resistors connected in series, both possessing equal resistances. Disregard any change in resistance due to temperature variations. Over a specific time interval,
options: an equal rise in temperature must occur in both resistors
an equal rise in temperature may occur in both resistors
the resistors must produce equal amounts of thermal energy
the resistors might produce unequal amounts of thermal energy
questions.solution:
The thermal energy produced in a resistor is given by $H = I^2Rt$.

Since both resistors are connected in series, the same current $I$ flows through them. Given that their resistances $R$ are equal, the thermal energy produced in both resistors over the same time interval $t$ must be equal.

The rise in temperature is given by $\Delta T = \dfrac{H}{ms}$, where $m$ is the mass and $s$ is the specific heat capacity of the resistor.

Since the material and mass of the two resistors are not specified, their heat capacities ($ms$) may or may not be equal. Therefore, an equal rise in temperature may occur, but it is not guaranteed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Thermal and Chemical Effects of Electric Current
questions.q: An electrolysis experiment is halted, and the connections to the battery terminals are subsequently reversed.
options: Heat will be generated at an increased rate.
The electrolysis process will cease.
The rate of material liberation will remain unchanged.
The rate at which material is liberated at the electrodes will increase.
questions.solution:
During electrolysis, a back EMF $e$ is developed in the cell due to the accumulation of products at the electrodes, which opposes the applied EMF $E$.

The effective EMF in the circuit is $E - e$, and the initial current is $I_1 = \dfrac{E - e}{R}$, where $R$ is the resistance of the circuit.

When the battery terminals are reversed, the applied EMF $E$ and the back EMF $e$ act in the same direction. The new effective EMF becomes $E + e$.

The new current in the circuit is $I_2 = \dfrac{E + e}{R}$.

Since $I_2 > I_1$, the magnitude of the current in the circuit increases.

The rate of heat generation is given by $I^2 R$. As the current increases, heat will be generated at an increased rate.

According to Faraday's first law of electrolysis, the rate of material liberation is $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = ZI$. Since the current increases, the rate at which material is liberated at the electrodes will also increase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Thermal and Chemical Effects of Electric Current
questions.q: The electrochemical equivalent of a material is dependent upon
options: the current passing through the electrolyte that contains the material
the nature of the material
the total amount of charge transferred through the electrolyte
the quantity of this material present within the electrolyte
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's first law of electrolysis, the mass $m$ of a substance deposited at an electrode is given by $m = ZQ$, where $Q$ is the charge and $Z$ is the electrochemical equivalent.

The electrochemical equivalent $Z$ is defined as the mass of the substance deposited by one coulomb of charge. It is given by the relation $Z = \dfrac{E}{F}$, where $E$ is the equivalent weight of the substance and $F$ is Faraday's constant.

Since the equivalent weight depends on the atomic weight and valency of the substance, the electrochemical equivalent is a characteristic property of the material.

Therefore, it depends only on the nature of the material and is independent of the current, total charge, or the quantity of the material present in the electrolyte.
